does: This paper proposes a machine unlearning method that uses a student-teacher framework with both competent and incompetent teachers to remove information about target data from trained neural networks without retraining. The competent teacher provides knowledge about data to retain while the incompetent teacher—trained to perform poorly on the forget set—guides the student toward forgetting specific classes or data. The authors experimentally demonstrate that their approach effectively removes information about forgotten data while preserving performance on retained data, and they introduce the zero retrain forgetting (ZRF) metric, which evaluates unlearning without requiring access to an expensive fully retrained reference model, making evaluation more practical and scalable across different settings.
connects: This work builds on the emerging area of machine unlearning that has gained importance due to privacy regulations like GDPR and the right to be forgotten. It extends prior student-teacher knowledge transfer frameworks—typically used for model compression and efficient learning—toward the novel goal of selective information removal. The paper responds to limitations in existing unlearning methods that often require expensive retraining or lack reliable evaluation metrics independent of a ground-truth retrained model. It connects to broader work on model forgetting, backdoor removal, and privacy-preserving machine learning by proposing that knowledge distillation principles can be inverted: rather than transferring correct knowledge, an incompetent teacher can actively guide forgetting of unwanted information while maintaining other learned representations.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of machine unlearning in production systems where retraining is prohibitively expensive. The ZRF metric provides researchers with an independent evaluation tool that doesn't require maintaining fully retrained models, accelerating research iteration and real-world deployment. The framework opens directions for more sophisticated teacher designs—potentially using ensemble methods or adversarial teachers—to target specific types of information removal. Future work could extend this approach to more complex forgetting scenarios like unlearning individual samples rather than classes, handling multi-modal data, or defending against inference attacks that try to recover forgotten information. The method could also inform privacy-preserving continual learning where models must forget outdated information while adapting to new tasks.
